How it Distinguishes (The "Fingerprint"): Low Centroid (< 150 Hz).Distinguishes it from the Snare, which has significant energy in the 200–500 Hz range and a higher centroid.
High Flatness (Noisy).The "rattle" of the snare wires creates white noise. This distinguishes it from Toms, which are more tonal (resonant/harmonic) and have lower flatness.
Lowest Fundamental Frequency (f0​).Distinguishes it from Mid/High Toms by detecting the specific pitch of the resonance.
Medium Fundamental Frequency.Identified by filtering for pitch bins higher than the Low Tom but lower than the High Tom.
Highest Fundamental Frequency.Distinguishes it from the Kick (which has no defined pitch or a dropping pitch) and lower toms.
Long Decay.After the onset, the energy stays high for >200ms. This distinguishes it from the Closed Hi-Hat, which decays almost instantly.
Short Decay (Staccato).Energy drops to near zero within <50ms. Distinguishes it from Crashes or Open Hats.
High Bandwidth (Wide Spread).The sound occupies a massive frequency range (noise). Distinguishes it from Ride (Sharp), which is more focused.
Tonal/Harmonic Content.The "ping" of a ride bell has a clear musical note. Distinguishes it from the Washy Ride or Crashes, which are purely atonal noise.
Highest Frequency Energy.Sustains high energy (>10kHz) for a long time. Distinguishes it from Hi-Hats, which have high energy but less volume/sustain.
Fastest Attack / High Transient.Often has a sharper initial spike than a washy crash. Distinguishes from Ride Wash by the violence of the initial hit.
Low Energy / Low Amplitude.The "chick" sound is spectrally similar to a closed hat but usually much quieter. Amplitude thresholding distinguishes this from a Stick Hit.
High Temporal Density.This is detected by the speed of events (e.g., <150ms between Kick classifications). Distinguishes a "Double Bass" passage from a standard Single Kick rhythm.
